Cultural Significance: The nine stars of Matariki mark the Māori new year. Each star has a domain: Matariki (mother), Pōhutukawa (the departed), Tupuānuku (earth food), Tupuārangi (sky food), Waipunaārangi (rain), Waitī (freshwater), Waitā (ocean), Ururangi (winds), Hiwaiterangi (wishes).
Navigation Use: Used for seasonal timing rather than navigation
Seasonal Marker: Heliacal rising in Pipiri signals the new year. Clarity of stars predicts the season ahead.

Cultural Significance: For some iwi (particularly South Island and Whanganui), Puanga rather than Matariki signals the new year. Blue-white light.
Navigation Use: Part of Tautoru / Orion region used for east-west orientation
Seasonal Marker: Rises just before Matariki — alternative new year marker

Cultural Significance: The brightest star in the night sky. Associated with the cold season — its name is also used for winter itself (te wā o Takurua).
Seasonal Marker: Dominant in winter sky; its prominence marks the coldest period

Cultural Significance: Three stars in a row — a key navigational and seasonal marker. Closely associated with Matariki and Puanga.
Navigation Use: Major navigation guide — east-west line; used for orientation at sea
Seasonal Marker: Visible throughout winter and early summer

Cultural Significance: The 'solitary one' — stands apart from other stars. Considered an ariki (chief) and guardian star. Used to predict yearly abundance.
Navigation Use: Second brightest star — prominent southern reference
Seasonal Marker: Observed to forecast whether the coming year will be plentiful

Cultural Significance: Star of summer, healing, and abundance. Reddish colour. Associated with warmth and the arrival of warmer months. Considered a star of great mana.
Navigation Use: Identifiable by red colour — marks eastern direction in certain seasons
Seasonal Marker: Rising prominence signals approach of warmer weather

Cultural Significance: Key navigation constellation visible year-round from Aotearoa. Used with pointer stars (Ngā Whatu / Te Punga) to find true south.
Navigation Use: Essential for finding south — the vertical arm of the cross points toward the south celestial pole
Seasonal Marker: Year-round reference; position relative to horizon indicates season

Cultural Significance: When Whānui rises with the sun in early spring, it signals the time for planting kūmara — one of the most important agricultural markers.
Navigation Use: Bright northern star, used for orientation
Seasonal Marker: Heliacal rising triggers kūmara planting season

Cultural Significance: Twin stars used for navigation and timing of seasonal activities.
Navigation Use: Pair used for navigation — identifying north

Cultural Significance: Known as 'the long fish' (Te Ikaroa) or the 'long shark' (Mangōroa). The great river of stars that spans the sky.
Navigation Use: The band itself provides broad orientation — its position indicates season and time of night
Seasonal Marker: Its orientation shifts across seasons — experienced navigators read its angle
